42:     get_flash_attn_version,
43:     is_flash_attn_varlen_func_available,
44: )
45: from vllm.v1.attention.backends.utils import split_decodes_and_prefills
46: from vllm.v1.attention.ops.triton_turboquant_decode import (
47:     _tq_full_dequant_kv,
48:     _use_fp8_e4b15,
49:     triton_turboquant_decode_attention,
50: )
51: from vllm.v1.attention.ops.triton_turboquant_store import triton_turboquant_store
52: 
53: _HAS_FLASH_ATTN = is_flash_attn_varlen_func_available()
54: if _HAS_FLASH_ATTN:
55:     from vllm.v1.attention.backends.fa_utils import flash_attn_varlen_func
56: 
57: # Continuation prefill: for small continuation chunks (q_len ≤ threshold),
58: # use the TQ decode kernel directly instead of full-dequant + flash_attn.
59: # do_kv_cache_update already stored all tokens to TQ cache, so the decode
60: # kernel can read them efficiently. This avoids O(cached_len) dequant work
61: # per continuation, eliminating the O(N²/chunk_size) collapse at long context.
62: _CONTINUATION_DECODE_THRESHOLD = 128
63: 
64: 
65: def _build_hadamard(d: int, device_str: str) -> torch.Tensor:
66:     """Orthonormal Hadamard matrix (Sylvester construction), cached per (d, device).
67: 
68:     Precomputed D×D matrix enables matmul-based WHT — single cuBLAS GEMM
69:     instead of log2(D) butterfly kernel launches. 64KB for D=128.
70:     """
71:     # Normalize device string so "cuda" and "cuda:0" hit the same cache entry.
72:     return _build_hadamard_cached(d, str(torch.device(device_str)))
73: 
74: 
75: @functools.cache
76: def _build_hadamard_cached(d: int, device_str: str) -> torch.Tensor:
77:     H = torch.tensor([[1.0]])
78:     while H.shape[0] < d:
79:         H = torch.cat([torch.cat([H, H], 1), torch.cat([H, -H], 1)], 0)
80:     return (H / math.sqrt(d)).to(torch.device(device_str))
81: 
82: 
83: class TurboQuantAttentionBackend(AttentionBackend):
84:     """Attention backend using TurboQuant KV-cache compression."""
85: 
86:     accept_output_buffer: bool = True
87:     forward_includes_kv_cache_update: bool = False
88: 
89:     supported_dtypes: ClassVar[list[torch.dtype]] = [
90:         torch.float16,
91:         torch.bfloat16,
92:     ]
93:     supported_kv_cache_dtypes: ClassVar[list[CacheDType]] = [
94:         "turboquant_k8v4",
95:         "turboquant_4bit_nc",
96:         "turboquant_k3v4_nc",
97:         "turboquant_3bit_nc",
98:     ]
99: 
100:     @staticmethod
101:     def get_name() -> str:
102:         return "TURBOQUANT"
103: 
104:     @staticmethod
105:     def get_supported_kernel_block_sizes() -> list[int | MultipleOf]:
106:         return [16, 32, 64, 128]
107: 
108:     @classmethod
109:     def supports_attn_type(cls, attn_type: str) -> bool:
110:         return attn_type == AttentionType.DECODER
111: 
112:     @classmethod
113:     def supports_per_head_quant_scales(cls) -> bool:
114:         return False
115: 
116:     @staticmethod
117:     def get_impl_cls() -> type["TurboQuantAttentionImpl"]:
118:         return TurboQuantAttentionImpl
119: 
120:     @staticmethod
121:     def get_builder_cls() -> type["TurboQuantMetadataBuilder"]:
122:         return TurboQuantMetadataBuilder
123: 
124:     @staticmethod
125:     def get_kv_cache_shape(
126:         num_blocks: int,
127:         block_size: int,
128:         num_kv_heads: int,
129:         head_size: int,
130:         cache_dtype_str: str = "turboquant_4bit_nc",
131:     ) -> tuple[int, ...]:
132:         """Combined K+V cache shape — no leading 2 dimension.
133: 
134:         Standard attention backends use (2, num_blocks, block_size, num_kv_heads,
135:         head_dim) with a leading 2 to separate K and V. TurboQuant packs K+V
136:         into a single interleaved slot per head per position, so the cache is:
137: 
138:             (num_blocks, block_size, num_kv_heads, slot_size_aligned)
139: 
140:         Each slot = [key_packed | value_packed | padding].
141:         This is safe because TQ has its own get_kv_cache_shape override and
142:         never shares cache tensors with other backends. Layers that fall back
143:         to native dtype via kv_cache_dtype_skip_layers get their own
144:         standard-shaped cache allocation.
145: 
146:         head_size is the model's real head_dim. slot_size_aligned is computed
147:         from the TQ config to ensure correct cache allocation for all head dims.
148:         """
149:         from vllm.model_executor.layers.quantization.turboquant.config import (
150:             TurboQuantConfig,
151:         )
152: 
153:         tq_config = TurboQuantConfig.from_cache_dtype(cache_dtype_str, head_size)
154:         return (num_blocks, block_size, num_kv_heads, tq_config.slot_size_aligned)
155: 
156:     @classmethod
157:     def supports_kv_cache_dtype(cls, kv_cache_dtype: CacheDType | None) -> bool:
158:         if kv_cache_dtype is None:
159:             return False
160:         return kv_cache_dtype.startswith("turboquant_")
161: 
162:     @classmethod
163:     def supports_head_size(cls, head_size: int) -> bool:
164:         # head_size from spec is effective_head_size (padded_slot//2),
165:         # not the model's actual head_dim. Accept any positive value.
166:         return head_size > 0
167: 
168: 
169: @dataclass
170: class TurboQuantMetadata(AttentionMetadata):
171:     """Metadata for TurboQuant attention."""
172: 
173:     seq_lens: torch.Tensor  # (num_reqs,) — total context length per request
174:     slot_mapping: torch.Tensor  # (num_tokens,) — cache slot for each token
175:     block_table: torch.Tensor  # (num_reqs, max_num_blocks)
176:     query_start_loc: torch.Tensor  # (num_reqs + 1,) — cu_seqlens for queries
177:     num_actual_tokens: int = 0  # actual tokens (excluding padding)
178:     max_query_len: int = 0  # longest query in batch
179:     max_seq_len: int = 0  # longest context in batch
180:     is_prefill: bool = False
181:     num_decodes: int = 0  # number of decode requests (first in batch)
182:     num_decode_tokens: int = 0  # tokens from decode requests
183: 
184: 
185: class TurboQuantMetadataBuilder(AttentionMetadataBuilder[TurboQuantMetadata]):
186:     """Builds TurboQuantMetadata from scheduler output."""
187: 
188:     _cudagraph_support: ClassVar[AttentionCGSupport] = AttentionCGSupport.UNIFORM_BATCH
189: 
190:     def __init__(self, kv_cache_spec, layer_names, vllm_config, device):
191:         super().__init__(kv_cache_spec, layer_names, vllm_config, device)
192:         self._init_reorder_batch_threshold(1, supports_spec_as_decode=False)
193: 
194:     def build_for_cudagraph_capture(
195:         self, common_attn_metadata: CommonAttentionMetadata
196:     ) -> TurboQuantMetadata:
197:         attn_metadata = self.build(0, common_attn_metadata)
198:         # Set seq_lens to 1 so CUDA graph capture is fast
199:         # (real seq_lens are filled at replay time).
200:         attn_metadata.seq_lens.fill_(1)
201:         return attn_metadata
202: 
203:     def build(self, common_prefix_len, common_attn_metadata, fast_build=False):
204:         """Build TurboQuantMetadata from common attention metadata."""
205:         cam = common_attn_metadata
206: 
207:         # With reorder_batch_threshold=1, the model runner guarantees
208:         # decodes come first in the batch. split_decodes_and_prefills
209:         # finds the boundary (operates on CPU tensors — no GPU sync).
210:         assert self.reorder_batch_threshold is not None
211:         num_decodes, num_prefills, num_decode_tokens, _ = split_decodes_and_prefills(
212:             cam, decode_threshold=self.reorder_batch_threshold
213:         )
214: 
215:         return TurboQuantMetadata(
216:             seq_lens=cam.seq_lens,
217:             slot_mapping=cam.slot_mapping,
218:             block_table=cam.block_table_tensor,
219:             query_start_loc=cam.query_start_loc,
220:             num_actual_tokens=cam.num_actual_tokens,
221:             max_query_len=cam.max_query_len,
222:             max_seq_len=cam.max_seq_len,
223:             is_prefill=(cam.max_query_len > 1),
224:             num_decodes=num_decodes,
225:             num_decode_tokens=num_decode_tokens,
226:         )
227: 
228: 
229: class TurboQuantAttentionImpl(AttentionImpl["TurboQuantMetadata"]):
230:     """TurboQuant attention implementation.
231: 
232:     Vectorized PyTorch: batch quantize/store, vectorized bit-unpack
233:     decode with einsum scores and value gather.
234:     """
235: 
236:     supports_quant_query_input: bool = False
237: 
238:     def __init__(
239:         self,
240:         num_heads: int,
241:         head_size: int,
242:         scale: float,
243:         num_kv_heads: int | None = None,
244:         alibi_slopes: list[float] | None = None,
245:         sliding_window: int | None = None,
246:         kv_cache_dtype: str = "auto",
247:         logits_soft_cap: float | None = None,
248:         attn_type: str = AttentionType.DECODER,
249:         kv_sharing_target_layer_name: str | None = None,
250:         **kwargs,
251:     ):
252:         self.num_heads = num_heads
253:         self.head_size = head_size
254:         self.scale = scale
255:         self.num_kv_heads = num_kv_heads if num_kv_heads is not None else num_heads
256:         self.num_kv_groups = num_heads // self.num_kv_heads
257:         self.kv_cache_dtype = kv_cache_dtype
258: 
259:         from vllm.model_executor.layers.quantization.turboquant.config import (
260:             TurboQuantConfig,
261:         )
262: 
263:         self.tq_config = TurboQuantConfig.from_cache_dtype(kv_cache_dtype, head_size)
264: 
265:         # Pre-compute kernel constants from config (avoid repeated arithmetic)
266:         cfg = self.tq_config
267:         self._mse_bytes = (
268:             math.ceil(head_size * cfg.key_mse_bits / 8)
269:             if not cfg.key_fp8
270:             else head_size
271:         )
272:         self._val_data_bytes = math.ceil(head_size * cfg.effective_value_quant_bits / 8)
273:         self._n_centroids = cfg.n_centroids if not cfg.key_fp8 else 1
274: 
275:         # Detect flash-attn version (FA2/3/4) for prefill paths.
276:         self.fa_version = get_flash_attn_version(head_size=head_size)
277: 
278:         # Fixed NUM_KV_SPLITS (grid dims must be constant for cudagraph,
279:         # and benchmarks show no regression vs dynamic in eager mode).
280:         vllm_config = get_current_vllm_config()
281:         self.max_num_kv_splits = (
282:             vllm_config.attention_config.tq_max_kv_splits_for_cuda_graph
283:         )
284: 
285:     def _flash_attn_varlen(
286:         self,
287:         q: torch.Tensor,
288:         k: torch.Tensor,
289:         v: torch.Tensor,
290:         cu_seqlens_q: torch.Tensor,
291:         cu_seqlens_k: torch.Tensor,
292:         max_seqlen_q: int,
293:         max_seqlen_k: int,
294:     ) -> torch.Tensor:
295:         # fa_utils.get_flash_attn_version() returns None on backends that
296:         # should not pass an explicit fa_version kwarg.
297:         if self.fa_version is None:
298:             return flash_attn_varlen_func(
299:                 q=q,
300:                 k=k,
301:                 v=v,
302:                 cu_seqlens_q=cu_seqlens_q,
303:                 cu_seqlens_k=cu_seqlens_k,

305:                 max_seqlen_k=max_seqlen_k,

This module needs PyTorch implementations of:

### Bit packing (for store path):
1. `pack_4bit(indices: Tensor[..., D]) -> Tensor[..., D//2]` - pack pairs of 4-bit values into bytes
2. `pack_3bit(indices: Tensor[..., D]) -> Tensor[..., D//8 * 3]` - pack groups of 8 3-bit values into 3 bytes
3. `pack_mse_4bit(indices: Tensor[..., D]) -> Tensor[..., D//2]` - same as pack_4bit but for MSE key indices
4. `pack_mse_3bit(indices: Tensor[..., D]) -> Tensor[..., D//8 * 3]` - same as pack_3bit but for MSE key indices

Actually, the packing is the same regardless of whether it's MSE key indices or value indices. The difference is just the bit width. So:
- `pack_4bit` packs pairs of 4-bit values (shifts [0, 4])
- `pack_3bit` packs groups of 8 3-bit values (shifts [0, 3, 6, 9, 12, 15, 18, 21])

### Bit unpacking (for dequant path):
1. `unpack_4bit(packed: Tensor[..., D//2]) -> Tensor[..., D]` - unpack 4-bit values
2. `unpack_3bit(packed: Tensor[..., D//8 * 3]) -> Tensor[..., D]` - unpack 3-bit values

### Store functions:
1. `store_tq_mse(key, value, kv_cache, slot_mapping, PiT, midpoints, mse_bits, key_packed_size, value_quant_bits)` - MSE key path
2. `store_tq_fp8(key, value, kv_cache, slot_mapping, key_packed_size, value_quant_bits)` - FP8 key path (but we're deferring FP8 to MSE 4-bit fallback)

### Dequant functions:
1. `dequant_tq_kv(kv_cache, block_table, centroids, Pi, mse_bits, key_packed_size, value_quant_bits, key_fp8, norm_correction)` - full dequant to float K/V buffers

Let me think about the exact implementation.

For the **store path** (MSE keys):
1. Flatten key to (NH, D), compute norms, normalize, rotate: `y = (k_flat / norms) @ PiT`
2. Bucketize: `idx = torch.bucketize(y, midpoints)` - this gives indices [0, n_centroids-1]
3. Pack MSE indices (3-bit or 4-bit)
4. Store norm as fp16 → 2 bytes
5. Quantize values: min/max per vector, scale = (max-min)/levels, q = round((v-min)/scale), pack
6. Store scale+zero as fp16 → 4 bytes
7. Scatter all packed bytes into kv_cache at slot positions

For the **dequant path**:
1. Gather packed bytes from kv_cache at slot positions
2. Unpack MSE indices, gather centroids, multiply by norm
3. Optional norm correction: renormalize centroid vector
4. Inverse rotate: `k = k @ Pi`
5. Unpack value indices, multiply by scale, add zero

Let me think about how to vectorize this efficiently.

### Store implementation (vectorized PyTorch):

Input: key (N, Hk, D), value (N, Hk, D), kv_cache (num_blocks, block_size, Hk, slot_size), slot_mapping (N,)

```python
def store_tq_mse(key, value, kv_cache, slot_mapping, PiT, midpoints, 
                 mse_bits, key_packed_size, value_quant_bits):
    N, Hk, D = key.shape
    NH = N * Hk
    block_size = kv_cache.shape[1]
    slot_size = kv_cache.shape[3]
    
    # Flatten to (NH, D)
    k_flat = key.float().reshape(NH, D)
    v_flat = value.float().reshape(NH, D)
    
    # Normalize + rotate
    norms = k_flat.norm(dim=1, keepdim=True)  # (NH, 1)
    x_hat = k_flat / (norms + 1e-8)
    y = x_hat @ PiT  # (NH, D)
    
    # Bucketize
    idx = torch.bucketize(y, midpoints)  # (NH, D), values in [0, n_centroids-1]
    
    # Expand slot_mapping for each head
    # slot_mapping is (N,), need (NH,)
    slot_per_head = slot_mapping.unsqueeze(1).expand(N, Hk).reshape(NH)  # (NH,)
    
    # Compute block and offset for each (token, head)
    blk = slot_per_head // block_size  # (NH,)
    off = slot_per_head % block_size  # (NH,)
    
    # Base byte offset into kv_cache for each (token, head)
    # kv_cache shape: (num_blocks, block_size, Hk, slot_size)
    # stride_block = block_size * Hk * slot_size
    # stride_pos = Hk * slot_size
    # stride_head = slot_size
    slot_base = blk * (block_size * Hk * slot_size) + off * (Hk * slot_size) + head_idx * slot_size
    
    # Actually, we need to be careful. The kv_cache is 4D. We can index it directly.
    # kv_cache[blk, off, head_idx, :] gives us the slot for this (token, head)
    # But blk, off, head_idx are per-(token,head) tensors of shape (NH,)
    
    # Actually, let's think about this differently.
    # For each token i and head h:
    #   slot = slot_mapping[i]
    #   blk = slot // block_size
    #   off = slot % block_size
    #   kv_cache[blk, off, h, :] is the target slot
    
    # We can use advanced indexing:
    head_idx = torch.arange(Hk, device=key.device).unsqueeze(0).expand(N, Hk).reshape(NH)
    # slot_per_head is (NH,) - same slot for all heads of a token
    blk = slot_per_head // block_size
    off = slot_per_head % block_size
    
    # Now kv_cache[blk, off, head_idx, :] gives (NH, slot_size)
    # But we need to write into specific byte ranges within each slot
    
    # Pack MSE indices
    if mse_bits == 4:
        mse_packed = pack_4bit(idx)  # (NH, D//2)
        mse_bytes = D // 2
    elif mse_bits == 3:
        mse_packed = pack_3bit(idx)  # (NH, D//8 * 3)
        mse_bytes = (D // 8) * 3  # = ceil(D * 3 / 8) when D is multiple of 8
    
    # Pack norm as fp16 bytes
    norm_f16 = norms.squeeze(1).half()  # (NH,)
    norm_bytes = norm_f16.view(torch.uint8).reshape(NH, 2)  # (NH, 2)
    
    # Quantize values
    v_min = v_flat.min(dim=1, keepdim=True).values  # (NH, 1)
    v_max = v_flat.max(dim=1, keepdim=True).values  # (NH, 1)
    levels = 2**value_quant_bits - 1
    v_scale = (v_max - v_min) / levels
    v_scale = torch.where(v_scale > 1e-8, v_scale, torch.full_like(v_scale, 1e-8))
    q_vals = torch.clamp(torch.round((v_flat - v_min) / v_scale), 0, levels).int()
    
    if value_quant_bits == 4:
        val_packed = pack_4bit(q_vals)  # (NH, D//2)
        val_data_bytes = D // 2
    elif value_quant_bits == 3:
        val_packed = pack_3bit(q_vals)  # (NH, D//8 * 3)
        val_data_bytes = (D // 8) * 3
    
    # Pack scale and zero as fp16 bytes
    scale_bytes = v_scale.squeeze(1).half().view(torch.uint8).reshape(NH, 2)
    zero_bytes = v_min.squeeze(1).half().view(torch.uint8).reshape(NH, 2)
    
    # Now scatter into kv_cache
    # The slot layout is: [mse_packed (mse_bytes) | norm (2) | val_packed (val_data_bytes) | scale (2) | zero (2)]
    # Total = mse_bytes + 2 + val_data_bytes + 4 = key_packed_size + value_packed_size
    
    # We can build the full slot as a (NH, slot_size) uint8 tensor and scatter
    slot_data = torch.zeros(NH, slot_size, dtype=torch.uint8, device=key.device)
    
    offset = 0
    slot_data[:, offset:offset + mse_bytes] = mse_packed
    offset += mse_bytes
    slot_data[:, offset:offset + 2] = norm_bytes
    offset += 2
    # offset should now be key_packed_size
    slot_data[:, offset:offset + val_data_bytes] = val_packed
    offset += val_data_bytes
    slot_data[:, offset:offset + 2] = scale_bytes
    offset += 2
    slot_data[:, offset:offset + 2] = zero_bytes
    
    # Scatter into kv_cache
    kv_cache[blk, off, head_idx, :] = slot_data
```

Wait, but the Triton kernel stores the norm at offset MSE_BYTES (which is ceil(D * mse_bits / 8)). And key_packed_size = mse_bytes + 2 (for the norm). So the layout is:
```
[mse_indices (mse_bytes) | norm (2 bytes) | val_data (val_data_bytes) | scale (2 bytes) | zero (2 bytes)]
```

And key_packed_size = mse_bytes + 2.
value_packed_size = val_data_bytes + 4.
slot_size = key_packed_size + value_packed_size.

This matches the config.py:
- `key_packed_size = mse_bytes + 2` (norm_bytes)
- `value_packed_size = val_data_bytes + 4` (scale + zero)
- `slot_size = key_packed_size + value_packed_size`

OK, so my layout is correct.

Now, about the fp16 byte order. The Triton kernel does:
```python
sc_u16 = sc_f16.to(tl.uint16, bitcast=True)
tl.store(..., (sc_u16 & 0xFF).to(tl.uint8))  # LSB
tl.store(..., ((sc_u16 >> 8) & 0xFF).to(tl.uint8))  # MSB
```

In PyTorch, `tensor.half().view(torch.uint8)` on a little-endian system gives [LSB, MSB], which matches. But I need to verify this is consistent on the NPU. Actually, for the dequant path, we just need to be consistent - read back the same way we wrote. So using `.view(torch.uint8)` should work.

Wait, actually there's a subtlety. When I do `v_scale.squeeze(1).half()`, this gives a (NH,) float16 tensor. Then `.view(torch.uint8)` gives (NH*2,) uint8. I need to reshape to (NH, 2). But the byte order depends on endianness. On x86 (little-endian), byte 0 is LSB, byte 1 is MSB, which matches the Triton kernel. On Ascend NPU... I need to check. But since we're writing and reading with the same code, it should be consistent.

Actually, let me think about this more carefully. The `.view(torch.uint8)` operation interprets the raw bytes of the float16 tensor. On a little-endian system, the first byte is the LSB. The Triton kernel stores LSB first. So they match on little-endian systems.

For the dequant path, we read the 2 bytes and reconstruct:
```python
u16 = lo | (hi << 8)
f16 = u16.view(torch.float16)  # or bitcast
```

In PyTorch, if I read 2 bytes as uint8 and want to reconstruct float16:
```python
bytes = kv_cache[..., :2]  # (NH, 2) uint8
u16 = bytes[:, 0].int() | (bytes[:, 1].int() << 8)
f16 = u16.short().view(torch.float16)
```

Wait, that's not quite right. Let me think again.

Actually, the simplest approach is:
```python
# Write: convert fp16 to 2 uint8 bytes
norm_f16 = norms.squeeze(1).half()  # (NH,) float16
norm_bytes = norm_f16.view(torch.uint8).reshape(NH, 2)  # (NH, 2) - [LSB, MSB] on little-endian

# Read: convert 2 uint8 bytes back to fp16
norm_bytes = kv_cache[blk, off, head_idx, mse_bytes:mse_bytes+2]  # (NH, 2) uint8
norm_f16 = norm_bytes.view(torch.float16).reshape(NH)  # (NH,) float16
```

This is simpler and guaranteed to be consistent since we use the same view operation for both read and write. The key insight is that `.view(torch.uint8)` and `.view(torch.float16)` are inverse operations on the same byte buffer, regardless of endianness.

But wait, there's a problem with advanced indexing. When I do:
```python
kv_cache[blk, off, head_idx, :2]  # this gives a copy, not a view
```

And I can't do `.view(torch.float16)` on a non-contiguous tensor. Let me think...

Actually, `kv_cache[blk, off, head_idx, :2]` returns a tensor of shape (NH, 2) which is contiguous (advanced indexing always creates a new contiguous tensor). So `.view(torch.float16)` should work... but wait, the last dimension is 2 uint8 = 2 bytes, and float16 is 2 bytes, so viewing (NH, 2) uint8 as (NH, 1) float16 then squeezing gives (NH,) float16. Yes, that works.

Actually wait, I need to be more careful. `kv_cache[blk, off, head_idx, :2]` gives shape (NH, 2). Viewing as float16: `(NH, 2).view(torch.float16)` - no, this doesn't work because 2 uint8 elements = 2 bytes = 1 float16 element. So I need `.view(NH, 1)` first, or `.reshape(NH, 2).view(torch.float16)` which would give (NH, 1) float16, then squeeze.

Hmm, actually `tensor.view(torch.float16)` changes the dtype but keeps the same number of elements if the element sizes are compatible... no, `.view(dtype)` reinterprets the raw bytes. So (NH, 2) uint8 = NH*2 bytes → (NH, 1) float16 = NH*2 bytes. But `.view(torch.float16)` on a (NH, 2) uint8 tensor would give (NH, 1) float16? No, `.view(dtype)` requires the total byte count to be divisible by the new element size. The shape stays the same in terms of bytes... actually no.

Let me think about this differently. In PyTorch:
```python
x = torch.zeros(4, 2, dtype=torch.uint8)
x.view(torch.float16)  # This gives shape (4, 1) float16
```

Yes, because 2 uint8 = 2 bytes = 1 float16, so the last dimension collapses from 2 to 1. But actually, I think `.view(torch.float16)` might not work this way. Let me reconsider.

Actually, `.view(dtype)` in PyTorch reinterprets the raw storage as the new dtype. The total number of bytes stays the same. So a (4, 2) uint8 tensor has 8 bytes total. Viewed as float16 (2 bytes each), that's 4 elements. The resulting shape would be (4, 1).

Hmm, actually I'm not 100% sure about the shape. Let me think about it differently. The `.view()` method preserves the number of elements... no, `.view(dtype)` is special. From the PyTorch docs: "View this tensor as the same data but a different type. ... The returned tensor shares the same underlying data with this tensor. ... The size of the last dimension will be adjusted based on the size of the new dtype."

So yes, (4, 2) uint8 viewed as float16 → (4, 1) float16. That's what I want.

OK so for reading:
```python

norm_f16 = norm_bytes.view(torch.float16).squeeze(-1)  # (NH,) float16
```

But wait - this requires the advanced-indexed result to be contiguous. Advanced indexing in PyTorch does produce contiguous results (when the indexed dimensions are not at the end, or when using all advanced indices). Actually, when all index tensors are used for the first N dimensions and a slice for the last, the result might not be contiguous in the way I need.

Actually, let me just use `.contiguous()` to be safe:
```python

norm_f16 = norm_bytes.view(torch.float16).squeeze(-1)
```

Hmm, but `.view(torch.float16)` on a (NH, 2) contiguous uint8 tensor... the resulting shape is (NH, 1). Then `.squeeze(-1)` gives (NH,). Good.

OK wait, I realize I'm overcomplicating this. Let me think about a cleaner approach.

For the **store** path, instead of building the full slot and doing a scatter, I can write each component directly:

```python
# Get the slot views
# kv_cache[blk, off, head_idx, :] gives (NH, slot_size)
slot_view = kv_cache[blk, off, head_idx, :]  # This is a copy, not a view!
# So I need to write back:
kv_cache[blk, off, head_idx, :] = slot_data
```

Actually, the cleanest approach is to build the full (NH, slot_size) uint8 tensor and do a single scatter. Let me do that.

For the **dequant** path, I need to gather the packed bytes, unpack them, and reconstruct K/V. Let me think about the dequant path:

```python
def dequant_tq_kv(kv_cache, block_table, centroids, Pi, 
                  seq_lens, mse_bits, key_packed_size, value_quant_bits,
                  key_fp8, norm_correction, block_size, head_dim, num_kv_heads):
    """Dequant cached K/V from TQ cache to float16 K/V buffers.
    
    Returns:
        k_out: (B, Hk, max_seq_len, D) float16
        v_out: (B, Hk, max_seq_len, D) float16
    """
    B = block_table.shape[0]
    max_seq_len = seq_lens.max().item()
    D = head_dim
    Hk = num_kv_heads
    device = kv_cache.device
    
    mse_bytes = math.ceil(D * mse_bits / 8) if not key_fp8 else 0
    val_data_bytes = math.ceil(D * value_quant_bits / 8)
    
    # For each batch, for each position, for each head:
    # Gather the slot bytes
    # pos: 0..max_seq_len-1
    # page_idx = pos // block_size
    # page_off = pos % block_size
    # block_num = block_table[b, page_idx]
    # slot = kv_cache[block_num, page_off, h, :]
    
    # Build index tensors
    pos = torch.arange(max_seq_len, device=device)
    page_idx = pos // block_size  # (max_seq_len,)
    page_off = pos % block_size  # (max_seq_len,)
    
    # block_table: (B, max_num_blocks)
    block_nums = block_table[:, page_idx]  # (B, max_seq_len)
    
    # For each (b, pos, h):
    # slot_bytes = kv_cache[block_nums[b, pos], page_off[pos], h, :]
    
    # Expand for all heads
    head_idx = torch.arange(Hk, device=device)  # (Hk,)
    # kv_cache[block_nums[:, :, None], page_off[None, :, None], head_idx[None, None, :], :]
    # gives (B, max_seq_len, Hk, slot_size)
    
    slot_bytes = kv_cache[block_nums[:, :, None], page_off[None, :, None], head_idx[None, None, :], :]
    # (B, max_seq_len, Hk, slot_size)
    
    # === K dequant ===
    if key_fp8:
        # FP8 path: first D bytes are FP8 keys
        k_raw = slot_bytes[..., :D]  # (B, max_seq_len, Hk, D) uint8
        k_out = k_raw.view(torch.float8_e4m3fn).to(torch.float16)
        # Hmm, but vllm uses float8e4nv or float8e4b15 depending on GPU
        # On Ascend, we don't have FP8 support yet, so this path is deferred
    else:
        # MSE path
        mse_packed = slot_bytes[..., :mse_bytes]  # (B, max_seq_len, Hk, mse_bytes)
        
        if mse_bits == 4:
            mse_idx = unpack_4bit(mse_packed)  # (B, max_seq_len, Hk, D)
        elif mse_bits == 3:
            mse_idx = unpack_3bit(mse_packed)  # (B, max_seq_len, Hk, D)
        
        # Gather centroids
        k_mse = centroids[mse_idx]  # (B, max_seq_len, Hk, D)
        
        # Norm correction
        if norm_correction:
            c_norm_sq = (k_mse ** 2).sum(dim=-1, keepdim=True)
            c_inv_norm = 1.0 / torch.sqrt(c_norm_sq + 1e-16)
            k_mse = k_mse * c_inv_norm
        
        # Read norm
        norm_bytes = slot_bytes[..., mse_bytes:mse_bytes+2]  # (B, max_seq_len, Hk, 2)
        vec_norm = norm_bytes.contiguous().view(torch.float16).squeeze(-1)  # (B, max_seq_len, Hk)
        
        # Reconstruct key
        k_recon = vec_norm.unsqueeze(-1) * k_mse  # (B, max_seq_len, Hk, D)
        
        # Inverse rotate: k @ Pi
        k_out = (k_recon.float() @ Pi).to(torch.float16)  # (B, max_seq_len, Hk, D)
    
    # === V dequant ===
    val_packed = slot_bytes[..., key_packed_size:key_packed_size+val_data_bytes]
    
    if value_quant_bits == 4:
        v_idx = unpack_4bit(val_packed)  # (B, max_seq_len, Hk, D)
    elif value_quant_bits == 3:
        v_idx = unpack_3bit(val_packed)  # (B, max_seq_len, Hk, D)
    
    # Read scale and zero
    sc_offset = key_packed_size + val_data_bytes
    scale_bytes = slot_bytes[..., sc_offset:sc_offset+2]
    v_scale = scale_bytes.contiguous().view(torch.float16).squeeze(-1)
    
    zero_bytes = slot_bytes[..., sc_offset+2:sc_offset+4]
    v_zero = zero_bytes.contiguous().view(torch.float16).squeeze(-1)
    
    v_out = v_idx.float() * v_scale.unsqueeze(-1) + v_zero.unsqueeze(-1)
    v_out = v_out.to(torch.float16)
    
    return k_out, v_out
```

Now for the pack/unpack functions:

### pack_4bit:
```python
def pack_4bit(indices: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:
    """Pack pairs of 4-bit values into bytes.
    
    Args:
        indices: (..., D) int tensor with values in [0, 15]
    Returns:
        (..., D//2) uint8 tensor
    """
    *batch, D = indices.shape
    idx = indices.int().reshape(*batch, D // 2, 2)
    shifts = torch.tensor([0, 4], device=indices.device, dtype=torch.int32)
    packed = ((idx & 0xF) << shifts).sum(dim=-1)
    return packed.to(torch.uint8)
```

### unpack_4bit:
```python
def unpack_4bit(packed: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:
    """Unpack 4-bit values from bytes.
    
    Args:
        packed: (..., D//2) uint8 tensor
    Returns:
        (..., D) int tensor with values in [0, 15]
    """
    *batch, D2 = packed.shape
    p = packed.int().unsqueeze(-1)  # (..., D//2, 1)
    shifts = torch.tensor([0, 4], device=packed.device, dtype=torch.int32)
    return ((p >> shifts) & 0xF)  # (..., D//2, 2)
    # Then reshape to (..., D)
```

Wait, I need to reshape. Let me be more careful:
```python
def unpack_4bit(packed: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:
    *batch, D2 = packed.shape
    p = packed.int().unsqueeze(-1).expand(*batch, D2, 2)  # (..., D//2, 2)
    shifts = torch.tensor([0, 4], device=packed.device, dtype=torch.int32).reshape(1, 1, 2)
    # Actually, broadcast: shifts shape (..., D//2, 2) - just need last dim to match
    result = (p >> shifts) & 0xF  # (..., D//2, 2)
    return result.reshape(*batch, D2 * 2)
```

Hmm, but `p >> shifts` where p is (..., D//2, 1) and shifts is (1, 1, 2)... no, I need to expand p to (..., D//2, 2) first, or use broadcasting.

Actually:
```python
p = packed.int()  # (..., D//2)
shifts = torch.tensor([0, 4], device=packed.device, dtype=torch.int32)  # (2,)
# p[..., None] >> shifts gives (..., D//2, 2) via broadcasting
result = (p[..., None] >> shifts) & 0xF  # (..., D//2, 2)
return result.reshape(*batch, D2 * 2)  # (..., D)
```

Wait, `p[..., None]` is (..., D//2, 1), and `shifts` is (2,). Broadcasting: (..., D//2, 1) >> (2,) → (..., D//2, 2). Yes, that works.

### pack_3bit:
Groups of 8 values → 3 bytes.

```python
def pack_3bit(indices: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:
    """Pack groups of 8 3-bit values into 3 bytes each.
    
    Args:
        indices: (..., D) int tensor with values in [0, 7], D must be multiple of 8
    Returns:
        (..., D//8 * 3) uint8 tensor
    """
    *batch, D = indices.shape
    idx = indices.int().reshape(*batch, D // 8, 8)
    shifts = torch.arange(8, device=indices.device, dtype=torch.int32) * 3  # (8,)
    packed_24 = ((idx & 0x7) << shifts).sum(dim=-1)  # (..., D//8) - 24-bit values
    
    b0 = (packed_24 & 0xFF).to(torch.uint8)
    b1 = ((packed_24 >> 8) & 0xFF).to(torch.uint8)
    b2 = ((packed_24 >> 16) & 0xFF).to(torch.uint8)
    
    # Interleave: [b0, b1, b2, b0, b1, b2, ...]
    result = torch.stack([b0, b1, b2], dim=-1)  # (..., D//8, 3)
    return result.reshape(*batch, (D // 8) * 3)
```

### unpack_3bit:
```python
def unpack_3bit(packed: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:
    """Unpack 3-bit values from bytes.
    
    Args:
        packed: (..., D//8 * 3) uint8 tensor
    Returns:
        (..., D) int tensor with values in [0, 7]
    """
    *batch, total_bytes = packed.shape
    n_groups = total_bytes // 3
    p = packed.int().reshape(*batch, n_groups, 3)
    
    b0 = p[..., 0]  # (..., n_groups)
    b1 = p[..., 1]
    b2 = p[..., 2]
    
    raw16 = b0 | (b1 << 8)  # (..., n_groups) - lower 16 bits
    # Actually, we need 24 bits: b0 | (b1 << 8) | (b2 << 16)
    raw24 = b0 | (b1 << 8) | (b2 << 16)  # (..., n_groups)
    
    shifts = torch.arange(8, device=packed.device, dtype=torch.int32) * 3  # (8,)
    # raw24[..., None] >> shifts gives (..., n_groups, 8)
    result = (raw24[..., None] >> shifts) & 0x7  # (..., n_groups, 8)
    return result.reshape(*batch, n_groups * 8)
```

Wait, I need to be careful about the Triton kernel's approach. Looking at the decode kernel:

For 3-bit MSE key unpack:
```python
mse_bit_off = d_offs * MSE_BITS  # d_offs * 3
mse_byte_idx = mse_bit_off // 8
mse_bit_shift = mse_bit_off % 8

mse_raw0 = load(KV_cache + slot_base + mse_byte_idx)  # byte at byte_idx
mse_raw1 = load(KV_cache + slot_base + mse_byte_idx + 1)  # byte at byte_idx+1
raw16 = mse_raw0 | (mse_raw1 << 8)
mse_idx = (raw16 >> mse_bit_shift) & mse_mask  # & 0x7 for 3-bit
```

So for element d:
- bit_offset = d * 3
- byte_idx = bit_offset // 8
- bit_shift = bit_offset % 8
- Read 2 bytes at byte_idx and byte_idx+1
- val = (raw16 >> bit_shift) & 0x7

This is a different approach from my group-of-8 approach. But they should give the same result since the packing is the same:
- 8 values, each 3 bits = 24 bits = 3 bytes
- Element 0: bits 0-2 of byte 0
- Element 1: bits 3-5 of byte 0
- Element 2: bits 6-7 of byte 0 + bit 0 of byte 1
- Element 3: bits 1-3 of byte 1
- etc.

My group-of-8 pack approach:
```python
shifts = [0, 3, 6, 9, 12, 15, 18, 21]
packed_24 = sum(idx[i] << shifts[i])
b0 = packed_24 & 0xFF  # bits 0-7
b1 = (packed_24 >> 8) & 0xFF  # bits 8-15
b2 = (packed_24 >> 16) & 0xFF  # bits 16-23
```

So byte 0 contains elements 0,1 and 2 bits of element 2. Byte 1 contains 1 bit of element 2, elements 3,4, and 1 bit of element 5. Etc.

The decode kernel reads 2 bytes and shifts, which is equivalent. Let me verify:
- Element 0: byte_idx=0, shift=0 → raw16[0:1] & 0x7 = bits 0-2 of byte 0 ✓
- Element 1: byte_idx=0, shift=3 → (raw16 >> 3) & 0x7 = bits 3-5 of byte 0 ✓
- Element 2: byte_idx=0, shift=6 → (raw16 >> 6) & 0x7 = bits 6-7 of byte 0 + bit 0 of byte 1 ✓

Yes, this matches. My unpack approach using the group-of-8 and shifts [0,3,6,9,12,15,18,21] is equivalent.

Actually wait, let me double check. In my unpack_3bit:
```python
raw24 = b0 | (b1 << 8) | (b2 << 16)
shifts = [0, 3, 6, 9, 12, 15, 18, 21]
result = (raw24 >> shifts) & 0x7
```

- Element 0: (raw24 >> 0) & 0x7 = bits 0-2 = b0[0:2] ✓
- Element 1: (raw24 >> 3) & 0x7 = bits 3-5 = b0[3:5] ✓
- Element 2: (raw24 >> 6) & 0x7 = bits 6-7 of b0 + bit 0 of b1 ✓

Great, this matches.

Now, there's an important consideration: the Triton kernel reads 2 bytes at a time (byte_idx and byte_idx+1), not 3 bytes. This means it might read across group boundaries. But that's fine because the bit-field extraction still works correctly - the 3-bit value either comes entirely from one byte or spans two bytes, and reading 2 consecutive bytes always captures it.

My group-based approach reads all 3 bytes at once, which is also correct but potentially different in terms of memory access patterns. For PyTorch on NPU, this shouldn't matter much.

Actually, the simplest approach: in the Ascend TQ backend's `__init__`, create a modified `TurboQuantConfig` that replaces FP8 with MSE 4-bit:

```python
if tq_config.key_fp8:
    # Ascend doesn't support FP8 KV cache yet; fall back to MSE 4-bit keys
    tq_config = TurboQuantConfig(
        head_dim=head_size,
        key_quant_bits=4,
        value_quant_bits=tq_config.value_quant_bits,
        norm_correction=False,  # k8v4 doesn't have NC
    )
```

This way, all the downstream code sees MSE 4-bit and works correctly. The cache layout changes (from head_dim bytes for FP8 to ceil(D*4/8)+2 bytes for MSE 4-bit), but since we control the allocation, this is fine.

This is a problem. Let me think about how to handle this...

Option 1: Don't override the config. Instead, handle FP8 in the ops by checking `key_fp8` and falling back to MSE 4-bit. But then the cache layout is FP8 (head_dim bytes for key) while I'm writing MSE 4-bit data (ceil(D*4/8)+2 bytes). This would overflow if head_dim < ceil(D*4/8)+2.

For D=128: head_dim=128, MSE 4-bit key_packed_size = 128/2 + 2 = 66. So MSE 4-bit needs 66 bytes but FP8 allocates 128 bytes. That's fine - we have extra space.

For D=64: head_dim=64, MSE 4-bit key_packed_size = 64/2 + 2 = 34. FP8 allocates 64 bytes. Fine.

So FP8 allocation is always >= MSE 4-bit allocation. We can write MSE 4-bit data into the FP8-allocated slot and just waste some bytes. But then during dequant, we need to know that it's actually MSE 4-bit, not FP8.

This is getting complicated. Let me take a different approach.

Option 2: Override the spec creation. Since we're on Ascend, we can modify the `get_kv_cache_spec` or the spec itself to use the correct slot size. But `get_kv_cache_spec` is in vllm's code, not Ascend's.

Option 3: In the Ascend TQ backend, always use the original config for slot size calculations (matching the spec), but internally use MSE 4-bit for the actual quantization. The key insight is that the slot has enough space (FP8 key takes D bytes, MSE 4-bit takes D/2+2 bytes, and D >= D/2+2 for D >= 4). So we can write MSE 4-bit data into the first D/2+2 bytes of the D-byte key region, and the remaining bytes are unused. During dequant, we read from the same first D/2+2 bytes.

But this means the `key_packed_size` used in the ops would be different from the `key_packed_size` in the config. The ops would need to know the "effective" key_packed_size (MSE 4-bit) vs the "allocated" key_packed_size (FP8).

This is doable but messy. Let me think of a cleaner way.

The Ascend code just uses the effective (MSE 4-bit) layout within the allocated space.

Actually, I think the cleanest approach is:

In the Ascend TQ backend `__init__`:
```python
self.tq_config = TurboQuantConfig.from_cache_dtype(kv_cache_dtype, head_size)
# FP8 key mode falls back to MSE 4-bit on Ascend (no FP8 KV cache support yet)
if self.tq_config.key_fp8:
    self.tq_config = TurboQuantConfig(
        head_dim=head_size,
        key_quant_bits=4,
        value_quant_bits=self.tq_config.value_quant_bits,
        norm_correction=False,
    )
```

And in the ops, use `self.tq_config` for all layout calculations. The allocated cache (based on the spec) will be larger than needed, but that's fine - we just use the first `effective_slot_size` bytes of each slot.

The key is: `slot_base` offset calculations use the *allocated* slot_size (from the cache tensor's stride), but within each slot, we use the *effective* layout (from the overridden config).

Wait, actually the slot_size in the kv_cache tensor is `tq_config.slot_size_aligned` from the spec, which uses the original config (FP8). The stride per head is this slot_size. So when we index `kv_cache[blk, off, head_idx, :]`, we get a slice of size `original_slot_size`, but we only write to the first `effective_slot_size` bytes.

For the value offset: in the original FP8 config, `key_packed_size = D` (FP8), and values start at offset D. In the effective MSE 4-bit config, `key_packed_size = D/2 + 2`, and values start at offset D/2+2.

So the value data in the FP8-allocated cache would be at offset D (if we followed the original layout), but in the MSE 4-bit effective layout, values start at D/2+2. These are different offsets!

This means I can't just use the effective config's offsets within the FP8-allocated slot. I need to either:
a) Use the effective layout (values at D/2+2), leaving bytes D/2+2 to D-1 unused between key and value. This wastes some space but works.
b) Use the original layout (values at D), which wastes even more space.

Option (a) is simpler: just use the effective config everywhere, and the allocated slot is larger than needed. The values start at `effective_key_packed_size` within the slot, regardless of the allocated slot size.

OK, I think this works. Let me just make sure: the `kv_cache` tensor has shape `(num_blocks, block_size, Hk, allocated_slot_size)`. When we access `kv_cache[blk, off, head_idx, :]`, we get a `allocated_slot_size`-byte slice. We write to the first `effective_slot_size` bytes. The remaining bytes are unused. During dequant, we read from the first `effective_slot_size` bytes. This is consistent and correct.

The only issue is wasted memory, but since FP8 mode is `turboquant_k8v4` which allocates D bytes for keys vs D/2+2 for MSE 4-bit, the waste is at most D/2-2 bytes per slot, which is acceptable.

OK great, I'll go with this approach. The ops functions will take the effective config parameters (mse_bits, key_packed_size, value_quant_bits, key_fp8=False always) and use them for all offset calculations. The allocated slot size is implicitly larger, but that doesn't affect correctness.

Let me also read the `_build_hadamard` function from vllm to replicate it. I already have it:

```python
@functools.cache
def _build_hadamard_cached(d: int, device_str: str) -> torch.Tensor:
    H = torch.tensor([[1.0]])
    while H.shape[0] < d:
        H = torch.cat([torch.cat([H, H], 1), torch.cat([H, -H], 1)], 0)
    return (H / math.sqrt(d)).to(torch.device(device_str))
```

This is pure PyTorch and should work on Ascend NPU. I'll include it in the ops module or in the scheme module.

- D7: reuse Sylvester Hadamard

Let me plan the ops module structure:

```python
# turboquant_ops.py

import math
import torch

# Hadamard construction
def build_hadamard(d, device) -> torch.Tensor: ...

# Bit packing
def pack_4bit(indices) -> torch.Tensor: ...
def unpack_4bit(packed) -> torch.Tensor: ...
def pack_3bit(indices) -> torch.Tensor: ...
def unpack_3bit(packed) -> torch.Tensor: ...

# fp16 <-> uint8 byte conversion helpers
def fp16_to_bytes(x) -> torch.Tensor: ...  # (...,) fp16 -> (..., 2) uint8
def bytes_to_fp16(x) -> torch.Tensor: ...  # (..., 2) uint8 -> (...,) fp16

# Store (quantize + pack + scatter into kv_cache)
def store_tq(key, value, kv_cache, slot_mapping, PiT, midpoints,
             mse_bits, key_packed_size, value_quant_bits, key_fp8=False): ...

# Full dequant (gather + unpack + dequant to float K/V)
def dequant_tq_kv(kv_cache, block_table, centroids, Pi,
                  max_seq_len, mse_bits, key_packed_size, value_quant_bits,
                  key_fp8=False, norm_correction=False,
                  block_size, head_dim, num_kv_heads): ...
```

Actually, looking at how the Triton store kernel works, the store function needs to handle:
1. MSE key path: normalize, rotate, bucketize, pack MSE indices, store norm, quantize+pack values, store scale/zero
2. FP8 key path: cast to FP8, store, quantize+pack values, store scale/zero

Since we're deferring FP8, I'll implement only the MSE path but structure it so FP8 can be added later.

Let me also think about the dequant function signature. Looking at the vllm TQ backend's `_continuation_prefill` and `_decode_attention`, the dequant is called with:
- kv_cache: (num_blocks, block_size, Hk, slot_size)
- block_table: (B, max_num_blocks)
- centroids: (n_centroids,) float32
- Pi: (D, D) float32
- max_seq_len or alloc_len: int

And returns:
- k_out: (B, Hk, max_seq_len, D) float16
- v_out: (B, Hk, max_seq_len, D) float16

Actually, looking more carefully at `_tq_full_dequant_kv`, it takes per-position, per-head, per-batch grid. The grid is `(alloc_len, 1 * Hk)` and bid = bh // Hk, hid = bh % Hk. So for each batch, it processes all positions and all heads.

Wait, actually the grid is `(alloc_len, B * Hk)` and `bid = bh // NUM_KV_HEADS, hid = bh % NUM_KV_HEADS`. So it processes `alloc_len * B * Hk` threads, each handling one position for one batch/head.

For the PyTorch version, I need to vectorize this. The key insight is that for each batch b, the block table maps positions to blocks. So I need to:

1. For each batch b and position p:
   - page_idx = p // block_size
   - page_off = p % block_size
   - block_num = block_table[b, page_idx]
   - slot = kv_cache[block_num, page_off, :, :]  # (Hk, slot_size)

2. Gather all slots into a (B, max_seq_len, Hk, slot_size) tensor.

3. Unpack and dequant as described above.

The gathering can be done with advanced indexing:
```python
B, max_blocks = block_table.shape
pos = torch.arange(max_seq_len, device=device)
page_idx = pos // block_size  # (max_seq_len,)
page_off = pos % block_size  # (max_seq_len,)
block_nums = block_table[:, page_idx]  # (B, max_seq_len)
head_idx = torch.arange(Hk, device=device)
# kv_cache[block_nums[:, :, None], page_off[None, :, None], head_idx[None, None, :], :]
# = (B, max_seq_len, Hk, slot_size)
slot_bytes = kv_cache[block_nums[:, :, None], page_off[None, :, None], head_idx[None, None, :], :]
```

This should work. The advanced indexing will gather the correct slots.

Now let me think about memory. For B=1, max_seq_len=8192, Hk=8, slot_size=612 (for k3v4_nc, D=256):
- 1 * 8192 * 8 * 612 = ~40M bytes = ~40MB

That's a lot for a single gather. But it's the same as what the Triton kernel processes, just done differently. The Triton kernel processes one position at a time, while PyTorch does it all at once. For large sequences, this might cause memory issues. But for now, let's go with the vectorized approach and optimize later if needed.

Actually, looking at the vllm TQ code, the dequant is only used for continuation prefill (large continuation chunks > 128 tokens). For decode, it uses the custom Triton kernel which does fused attention + dequant. On Ascend, we'll use FIA (Fused Infer Attention) for decode, which requires dequanting to float first. So the dequant function will be used for both decode and continuation prefill.

For decode, B is the batch size and max_seq_len is the max context length. This could be large (e.g., 8192). But the gathered tensor is (B, max_seq_len, Hk, slot_size), and we only need the K/V values, not the raw bytes. So the memory usage is proportional to the cache size, which is already allocated.

12: def _fa_quant_weight_loader(param: torch.Tensor, loaded_weight: torch.Tensor):
13:     """Weight loader for MLA-based C8 (FAKQuant) models."""
14:     if param.numel() == 1 and loaded_weight.numel() == 1:
15:         param.data.fill_(loaded_weight.item())
16:     else:
17:         tp_rank = get_tensor_model_parallel_rank()
18:         tp_size = get_tensor_model_parallel_world_size()
19:         shard_size = loaded_weight.shape[0] // tp_size
20:         loaded_weight = loaded_weight.narrow(0, shard_size * tp_rank, shard_size)
21:         assert param.size() == loaded_weight.size(), (
22:             "[vllm-ascend/FAKQuant] Attempted to load weight "
23:             f"({loaded_weight.size()}) into parameter ({param.size()}) "
24:             f"when TP size is {tp_size} and TP rank is {tp_rank}."
25:         )

45:         params_dict["fa_k.scale"] = torch.empty((layer.num_kv_heads, 1), dtype=dtype)
46:         params_dict["fa_v.scale"] = torch.empty((layer.num_kv_heads, 1), dtype=dtype)

55:             module.register_parameter(weight_name, weight_param)

108: class AscendC8KVCacheAttentionMethod(AscendAttentionScheme):
109:     """C8 INT8 KV cache quantization for dense-attention models (e.g. Qwen3)."""
110: 
111:     def __init__(self, quant_description: dict, prefix: str):
112:         self.quant_description = quant_description
113:         self.prefix = prefix
114:         vllm_config = get_current_vllm_config()
115:         self.is_kv_producer = False
116:         if vllm_config.kv_transfer_config is not None:
117:             self.is_kv_producer = vllm_config.kv_transfer_config.is_kv_producer
118: 
119:     def create_weights(self, layer: torch.nn.Module) -> None:
120:         # Returns int8 if the P node is not a PD detachment node.
121:         if not self.is_kv_producer:
122:             logger.info_once(
123:                 "[vllm-ascend/C8_KV] KV cache producer is disabled; setting kv_cache_torch_dtype to torch.int8."
124:             )
125:             layer.kv_cache_torch_dtype = torch.int8
126:         # Upgrade impl to the C8-specific subclass so the C8 forward path is always used.

138:         layer.v_cache_offset = torch.nn.Parameter(torch.zeros(1, dtype=dtype), requires_grad=False)
139:         layer.v_cache_offset.weight_loader = _c8_kv_scale_weight_loader
140: 
141:     def process_weights_after_loading(self, layer: torch.nn.Module) -> None:

143:         layer.k_cache_offset.data = layer.k_cache_offset.data.flatten()

# vLLM Ascend Development Guidelines

This document provides instructions for contributors to the vLLM Ascend project. Please read and follow these guidelines to ensure code quality, maintainability, and consistency.

---

## Table of Contents

- [Setup and Environment](#setup-and-environment)
    - [Environment Variables](#environment-variables)
    - [Environment Variable Review Requirement](#environment-variable-review-requirement)
- [Testing](#testing)
    - [Unit and System Tests](#unit-and-system-tests)
    - [Running Tests](#running-tests)
- [Code Style](#code-style)
    - [Python Conventions](#python-conventions)
    - [Naming Conventions](#naming-conventions)
- [NPU-Specific Considerations](#npu-specific-considerations)
    - [Tensor item() Operations](#tensor-item-operations)
    - [Memory and Performance](#memory-and-performance)
- [Model and Plugin Architecture](#model-and-plugin-architecture)
    - [vLLM Ascend Plugin Architecture](#vllm-ascend-plugin-architecture)
    - [Patching Requirement](#patching-requirement)
    - [Model Runner Changes](#model-runner-changes)
- [Commit Messages and Pull Requests](#commit-messages-and-pull-requests)
    - [Commit Message Format](#commit-message-format)
- [Review Checklist](#review-checklist)
    - [Code Quality](#code-quality)
    - [Testing](#testing-1)
    - [Documentation](#documentation)
    - [NPU Considerations](#npu-considerations)
    - [Commit and PR](#commit-and-pr)
- [Quick Start for Contributors](#quick-start-for-contributors)
- [References](#references)

---

## Setup and Environment

### Environment Variables

All environment variables must be defined in `vllm_ascend/envs.py` using the centralized `env_variables` dictionary.

**Requirements:**

- Add documentation for each environment variable in the `env_variables` dict comment
- Specify default values and valid ranges
- Indicate whether the variable is sensitive (credentials, keys)

**Example:**

```python
import os

env_variables = {
    "VLLM_ASCEND_ENABLE_NZ": lambda: int(os.getenv("VLLM_ASCEND_ENABLE_NZ", 1)),
    # ...
}
```

**Never**: Hardcode environment variable names throughout the codebase. Reference them from the central module using `from vllm_ascend import envs`.

### Environment Variable Review Requirement

**Strict Review Required**: All new environment variables must undergo code review.

Reviewers must verify:

- The variable name follows the `VLLM_ASCEND_*` naming convention
- Default value is appropriate for all supported hardware
- Documentation is added to the `env_variables` dict
- The variable is used in a performance-critical path

---

## Testing

### Unit and System Tests

**Requirement**: All new functionality requires corresponding tests.

- **Unit Tests (UT)**: Located in `tests/ut/`, cover core logic, edge cases, and error conditions
- **System Tests (ST)**: Located in `tests/e2e/`, verify end-to-end behavior and integration points
- **Nightly Tests**: Include benchmarks for NPU-specific code paths in `tests/e2e/nightly/`

**Test Coverage Guidelines:**

- New features: Tests must cover happy path and failure modes
- Bug fixes: Tests must include a regression test for the bug
- Performance-critical code: Include benchmarks and performance regression tests

### Running Tests

```bash


# Run specific unit test file
pytest -sv tests/ut/ops/test_prepare_finalize.py

# Run specific unit test
pytest -sv tests/ut/ops/test_prepare_finalize.py::test_prepare_inputs

# Run NPU-specific tests (requires NPU hardware)
pytest -sv tests/e2e/singlecard/test_piecewise_res_consistency.py
```

**Requirement**: Run all tests locally before requesting review. Verify tests pass on NPU hardware for NPU-specific changes.

---

## Code Style

### Python Conventions

- **Imports**: All imports at the top of the file. Valid exceptions:
    - Circular imports (use inline imports)
    - Lazy loading for worker/isolation processes
    - Type-checking imports wrapped in `if TYPE_CHECKING:`

- **Global Variables**: Avoid new global variables. Pass dependencies explicitly through function parameters.

    **Allowed:**
    - Constants named `ALL_UPPER_CASE` (e.g., `MAX_BATCH_SIZE` in `envs.py`)
    - Immutable configuration objects

    **Requires Approval:**
    - Any new mutable global state

- **No Magic Numbers**: Use named constants with descriptive names:

    ```python
    # Bad
    if seq_len > 2048: ...

    # Good
    MAX_CONTEXT_LENGTH = 2048
    if seq_len > MAX_CONTEXT_LENGTH: ...
    ```

- **Descriptive Naming**: Use names that describe functionality, not implementation details.

    ```python
    # Bad
    is_deepseek_v3_r1
    flag1
    tmp_var

    # Good
    supports_dynamic_temperature
    uses_speculative_decoding
    ```

### Naming Conventions

- **Classes**: `PascalCase` (e.g., `NPUModelRunner`, `AscendSampler`, `ACLGraphManager`)
- **Functions/Methods**: `snake_case` (e.g., `forward_pass`, `compute_attention`)
- **Constants**: `ALL_UPPER_CASE` (e.g., `MAX_BATCH_SIZE`, `VLLM_ASCEND_ENABLE_NZ`)
- **Variables**: `snake_case` (e.g., `token_ids`, `sequence_lengths`)

---

## NPU-Specific Considerations

### Tensor item() Operations

**Warning**: `tensor.item()` operations cause synchronization overhead on NPU when the `tensor` is on device.

If the `tensor` is a device tensor, calling `item()` will triggers a synchronous data transfer from NPU to CPU. This can severely degrade performance in hot paths, causing `AsyncScheduler` to block here.

**Review Requirements:**

1. Profile performance impact before merging
2. Consider alternative patterns:
    - Keep values on device when possible
    - Batch operations to reduce sync frequency
    - Use device-side operations (e.g., `torch.argmax`, `torch.sum`)
3. Document when `item()` is unavoidable (e.g., logging, conditional logic)

**Example Patterns:**

```python
# Bad: In hot loop - causes sync per iteration
for tensor in tensors:
    value = tensor.item()

# Better: Batch operations - single sync
values = [t.item() for t in tensors]  # Single batch sync

# Good: Keep on device when possible
max_value = torch.max(tensor)  # No sync needed
if max_value > threshold:  # Comparison can stay on device
    ...
```

### Memory and Performance

Additional NPU-specific best practices:

- Avoid CPU-NPU memory transfers in hot paths
- Prefer in-place operations where safe (e.g., `x.add_()`, `x.mul_()`)
- Monitor memory fragmentation, especially for long-running processes
- Test with realistic workloads on actual NPU hardware (Ascend 910B/C)

---

## Model and Plugin Architecture

### vLLM Ascend Plugin Architecture

vLLM Ascend is a **hardware plugin** that integrates with upstream vLLM via the pluggable hardware interface. It does not add new model files directly.

**Required Pattern**: Model-specific functionality should be implemented via:

1. **Patching** (in `vllm_ascend/patch/`):
    - `vllm_ascend/patch/platform/` - Platform-level patches (distributed, scheduling)
    - `vllm_ascend/patch/worker/` - Worker-level patches (model-specific behavior)
    - Example: `patch_deepseek.py` modifies upstream Deepseek model behavior
    - Patch is not the best solution for all cases. Use it when necessary.

2. **Inheritance**:
    - `NPUModelRunner(GPUModelRunner)` - Extend vLLM model runner with NPU-specific behavior
    - `AscendSampler` - Extend vLLM sampler with NPU-specific operations
    - Add NPU-specific components via composition (e.g., `AclGraphManager`)
    - Custom Operators - NPU-specific custom operators (e.g., `AscendRMSNorm`)

3. **External upstream contributions** where appropriate

### Patching Requirement

**Strict Review Required**: All new patches must undergo thorough architectural review.

Reviewers must verify:

- The patch targets the correct upstream component
- The patch is minimal and focused
- Performance implications are understood
- A long-term plan exists for upstream contribution

**Example Patch Pattern:**

```python
# vllm_ascend/patch/worker/patch_deepseek.py
from vllm.model_executor.models.deepseek_v2 import DeepseekV2Model

def forward(self, input_ids, positions, ...):
    # NPU-specific forward implementation
    ...

DeepseekV2Model.forward = forward  # Patch upstream class
```

### Model Runner Changes

**Strict Review Required**: All new behaviors added to `model_runner` must undergo thorough architectural review.

Reviewers must verify:

- The necessity of the new behavior (why can't this be in a patch?)
- Performance implications on NPU hardware
- Compatibility with existing model implementations
- Long-term maintainability and test coverage

**NPU Model Runner Files:**

- `vllm_ascend/worker/model_runner_v1.py` - vLLM v1 model runner
- `vllm_ascend/worker/v2/model_runner.py` - vLLM v2 model runner
- `vllm_ascend/_310p/model_runner_310p.py` - Ascend 310P model runner

---

## Commit Messages and Pull Requests

### Commit Message Format

Follow the [Conventional Commits](https://www.conventionalcommits.org/) format and **must include a sign-off**:

```bash
git commit -s -m "<type>: <summary>" -m "<body - explaining what changed and why>"
```

Or using the full message format:

```txt
<type>: <summary>

<body - explaining what changed and why>

Signed-off-by: Your Name <your.email@example.com>
```

**Valid Types**: `feat`, `fix`, `perf`, `refactor`, `test`, `docs`, `chore`

**Good Examples:**

```txt
feat(npu): add flash attention support for Ascend CANN

- Implements FlashAttention-2 kernel for NPU backend
- Reduces memory usage by 30% compared to baseline

fix(model_runner): correct padding token handling

- Fixes token padding that caused incorrect attention masks
- Addresses issue #1234

perf: avoid CPU-NPU sync in attention computation

- Inline computation to avoid tensor.item() calls
- Improves throughput by 15%
```

**Bad Examples:**

```txt
fix bug
add feature
update code
```

### Pull Request Title Format

PR titles should follow the format: `[Type][Module] Description`

- **Type**: The type of change (e.g., `CI`, `Doc`, `BugFix`, `Feat`, `Platform`, `Refactor`)
- **Module**: The affected module (optional, e.g., `Misc`, `Model`, `Worker`)
- **Description**: Brief description of the change

**Examples:**

- `[Doc][Misc] Update contribution guidelines`
- `[BugFix] Fix CPU binding logic`
- `[CI] Update image build workflow`

### Pull Request Template

When creating a PR, please follow the template in `.github/PULL_REQUEST_TEMPLATE.md` and ensure the following sections are completed:

> **Note**: The PR description will be automatically updated by GitHub Actions to include vLLM version info at the bottom. If you update the PR description via API or CLI, make sure to preserve the `- vLLM version:` and `- vLLM main:` lines.

- **What this PR does / why we need it?** - Clearly describe the changes and their purpose
- **Does this PR introduce _any_ user-facing change?** - Indicate if there are any user-visible changes
- **How was this patch tested?** - Describe how you tested the changes. Examples:
    - Unit tests added/updated: list the test files
    - Manual testing: provide the test steps and commands
    - CI testing: indicate if only CI verification is needed

---

## Review Checklist

Before merging, verify:

### Code Quality

- [ ] Code follows style guidelines (naming, imports, no magic numbers)
- [ ] No global state added without justification
- [ ] Patching pattern used correctly (if applicable)
- [ ] No direct model file additions

### Testing

- [ ] New tests added for new functionality (`tests/ut/` or `tests/e2e/`)
- [ ] Existing tests pass
- [ ] NPU-specific tests verified on actual hardware
- [ ] Performance benchmarks included where applicable

### Documentation

- [ ] Environment variables documented
- [ ] Public APIs documented
- [ ] User-facing changes reflected in docs

### NPU Considerations

- [ ] `tensor.item()` usage reviewed for performance impact
- [ ] No unnecessary CPU-NPU transfers in hot paths
- [ ] Memory usage verified on NPU hardware

### Commit and PR

- [ ] Commit messages are clear and descriptive, following Conventional Commits format
- [ ] **All commits are signed off** (`git commit -s`)
- [ ] PR is created from your fork repository, not directly from the main repository
- [ ] PR description is complete, following the PR template
- [ ] All review comments addressed

---

## Quick Start for Contributors

1. Install development dependencies: `pip install -e .[dev]`
2. Run tests: `pytest tests/`
3. Check linting: `ruff check vllm_ascend/`
4. Format code: `ruff format vllm_ascend/`
5. Make your changes following guidelines in this document
6. Add tests for new behavior
7. Run full test suite before committing
8. Commit with sign-off: `git commit -s`
9. Run linting check before pushing:
   ```bash
   bash format.sh ci
   ```
   > **Note**: This check is required for **all file types**, including markdown files. If `markdownlint` modifies files, re-add them with `git add` and commit again.
10. Push to your fork repository (NOT the main repository):

   ```bash
   git remote add myfork https://github.com/YOUR_USERNAME/vllm-ascend.git
   git push -u myfork your-branch-name
   ```

11. Create a PR from your fork to the main repository with clear description

---

## References

- [vLLM Hardware Plugin RFC](https://github.com/vllm-project/vllm/issues/11162)
- [Documentation](https://docs.vllm.ai/projects/ascend/en/latest/)
- [Contributors Guide](https://docs.vllm.ai/projects/ascend/en/latest/community/contributors.html)

</system-reminder>

1445:         def _shard_and_reshape(raw: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:
1446:             if raw.numel() == 1:
1447:                 return raw.to(device=device)
1448:             expected = self.num_kv_heads * self.head_size

1455:                     kv_head_start : kv_head_start + self.num_kv_heads

1475:     def _dequant_paged_kv_to_dense(
1476:         self,
1477:         key: torch.Tensor,
1478:         value: torch.Tensor,
1479:         block_table: torch.Tensor,
1480:         seq_lens: list,
1481:         target_dtype: torch.dtype,
1482:         layer,
1483:     ) -> tuple[torch.Tensor, torch.Tensor]:
1484:         """Gather paged INT8 KV blocks and dequantize."""
1485:         batch_size = block_table.shape[0]
1486:         block_size = key.shape[1]
1487:         H = key.shape[2]
1488:         max_blocks_per_seq = block_table.shape[1]
1489:         max_tokens_padded = max_blocks_per_seq * block_size
1490: 
1491:         flat_ids = block_table.reshape(-1)
1492:         gathered_k = key[flat_ids].view(batch_size, max_tokens_padded, H)
1493:         gathered_v = value[flat_ids].view(batch_size, max_tokens_padded, H)
1494: 
1495:         seq_lens_t = torch.tensor(seq_lens, dtype=torch.long, device=key.device)
1496:         positions = torch.arange(max_tokens_padded, dtype=torch.long, device=key.device)
1497:         valid_mask = (positions.unsqueeze(0) < seq_lens_t.unsqueeze(1)).view(-1)
1498: 
1499:         dense_k = gathered_k.view(-1, H)[valid_mask]
1500:         dense_v = gathered_v.view(-1, H)[valid_mask]
1501: 
1502:         dense_k = dense_k.view(-1, self.num_kv_heads, self.head_size)
1503:         dense_v = dense_v.view(-1, self.num_kv_heads, self.head_size)
1504:         dense_k = (dense_k.to(target_dtype) - layer._c8_k_offset) * layer._c8_k_scale
1505:         dense_v = (dense_v.to(target_dtype) - layer._c8_v_offset) * layer._c8_v_scale
1506:         return dense_k, dense_v
1507: 
1508:     def _quantize_kv_to_int8(
1509:         self,
1510:         key: torch.Tensor,
1511:         value: torch.Tensor,
1512:         layer: AttentionLayer,
1513:         num_actual_tokens: int,
1514:     ) -> tuple[torch.Tensor, torch.Tensor]:
1515:         """Quantize K/V from float to INT8 using static per-channel C8 scales."""
1516:         actual_key = key[:num_actual_tokens]
1517:         actual_value = value[:num_actual_tokens]
1518: 
1519:         k_int8 = torch.clamp(
1520:             torch.round(actual_key * layer._c8_k_inv_scale + layer._c8_k_offset),
1521:             -128,
1522:             127,
1523:         ).to(torch.int8)
1524:         v_int8 = torch.clamp(
1525:             torch.round(actual_value * layer._c8_v_inv_scale + layer._c8_v_offset),
1526:             -128,
1527:             127,
1528:         ).to(torch.int8)
1529:         return k_int8, v_int8

1568:     def _forward_c8_chunked_prefill(
1569:         self,
1570:         query: torch.Tensor,
1571:         float_key: torch.Tensor | None,
1572:         float_value: torch.Tensor | None,
1573:         attn_metadata: AscendMetadata,
1574:         output: torch.Tensor,
1575:         layer: AttentionLayer,
1576:     ) -> torch.Tensor:
1577:         """C8 ChunkedPrefill: decode via FIA V1 BNSD paged INT8 (zero gather),
1578:         prefill via FIA V1 TND with float KV (new) or gather+dequant (continuing).
1579:         """
1580:         num_decode_tokens = attn_metadata.num_decode_tokens
1581:         num_decodes = attn_metadata.num_decodes
1582:         actual_seq_qlen = attn_metadata.actual_seq_lengths_q
1583:         num_tokens = int(actual_seq_qlen[-1])  # type: ignore[index]
1584: 
1585:         if num_decode_tokens > 0:
1586:             num_block, block_size, _, _ = self.key_cache.shape  # type: ignore[attr-defined]
1587:             assert block_size % 32 == 0, (
1588:                 f"C8 INT8 KV cache requires block_size to be a multiple of 32, got {block_size}"
1589:             )
1590:             kv_k = self.key_cache.view(num_block, block_size, -1)  # type: ignore[attr-defined]
1591:             kv_v = self.value_cache.view(num_block, block_size, -1)  # type: ignore[attr-defined]
1592: 
1593:             attn_out, _ = torch_npu.npu_fused_infer_attention_score(
1594:                 query[:num_decode_tokens].unsqueeze(2),
1595:                 kv_k,
1596:                 kv_v,
1597:                 key_antiquant_scale=layer._c8_k_aq_scale,
1598:                 key_antiquant_offset=layer._c8_k_aq_offset,
1599:                 value_antiquant_scale=layer._c8_v_aq_scale,
1600:                 value_antiquant_offset=layer._c8_v_aq_offset,
1601:                 block_table=attn_metadata.block_tables[:num_decodes],
1602:                 actual_seq_lengths_kv=attn_metadata.seq_lens_list[:num_decodes],
1603:                 num_heads=self.num_heads,
1604:                 num_key_value_heads=self.num_kv_heads,
1605:                 input_layout="BNSD",
1606:                 scale=self.scale,
1607:                 block_size=block_size,
1608:                 key_antiquant_mode=0,
1609:                 value_antiquant_mode=0,
1610:                 sparse_mode=0,
1611:             )
1612:             output[:num_decode_tokens] = attn_out.squeeze(2)
1613: 
1614:         if attn_metadata.num_prefills > 0:
1615:             prefill_q = query[num_decode_tokens:num_tokens]
1616: 
1617:             prefill_seq_qlen = [
1618:                 actual_seq_qlen[i] - num_decode_tokens for i in range(num_decodes, len(actual_seq_qlen))
1619:             ]
1620: 
1621:             all_new_prefill = True
1622:             for i in range(num_decodes, len(attn_metadata.seq_lens_list)):
1623:                 q_start = actual_seq_qlen[i - 1] if i > 0 else 0
1624:                 qlen_i = actual_seq_qlen[i] - q_start
1625:                 if attn_metadata.seq_lens_list[i] > qlen_i:
1626:                     all_new_prefill = False
1627:                     break
1628: 
1629:             if all_new_prefill and float_key is not None and float_value is not None:
1630:                 prefill_k = float_key[num_decode_tokens:num_tokens]
1631:                 prefill_v = float_value[num_decode_tokens:num_tokens]
1632:                 prefill_seq_kvlen = prefill_seq_qlen
1633:             else:
1634:                 num_block, blk_size, _, _ = self.key_cache.shape  # type: ignore[attr-defined]
1635:                 paged_k = self.key_cache.view(num_block, blk_size, -1)  # type: ignore[attr-defined]
1636:                 paged_v = self.value_cache.view(num_block, blk_size, -1)  # type: ignore[attr-defined]
1637:                 prefill_bt = attn_metadata.block_tables[num_decodes:]
1638:                 prefill_sl = attn_metadata.seq_lens_list[num_decodes:]
1639:                 prefill_k, prefill_v = self._dequant_paged_kv_to_dense(
1640:                     paged_k, paged_v, prefill_bt, prefill_sl, query.dtype, layer
1641:                 )
1642:                 prefill_seq_kvlen = torch.tensor(prefill_sl, dtype=torch.int32).cumsum(dim=0)
1643: 
1644:             # block_table is None for prefill; FIA ignores block_size in this case.
1645:             # Use cache block_size for consistency rather than a magic number.
1646:             cache_block_size = self.key_cache.shape[1]  # type: ignore[attr-defined]
1647:             attn_out, _ = torch_npu.npu_fused_infer_attention_score(
1648:                 query=prefill_q,
1649:                 key=prefill_k,
1650:                 value=prefill_v,
1651:                 atten_mask=attn_metadata.attn_mask,
1652:                 block_table=None,
1653:                 input_layout="TND",
1654:                 block_size=cache_block_size,
1655:                 actual_seq_lengths=prefill_seq_qlen,
1656:                 actual_seq_lengths_kv=prefill_seq_kvlen,
1657:                 num_key_value_heads=self.num_kv_heads,
1658:                 num_heads=self.num_heads,
1659:                 scale=self.scale,
1660:                 sparse_mode=3,
1661:             )
1662:             n_prefill = num_tokens - num_decode_tokens
1663:             attn_out = attn_out.view(n_prefill, self.num_heads, self.head_size)
1664:             output[num_decode_tokens:num_tokens] = attn_out[:n_prefill]
1665: 
1666:         return output
1667: 
1668:     def _forward_c8_fused_infer_attention(
1669:         self,
1670:         query: torch.Tensor,
1671:         key: torch.Tensor,
1672:         value: torch.Tensor,
1673:         attn_metadata: AscendMetadata,
1674:         output: torch.Tensor,
1675:         layer: AttentionLayer,
1676:     ):
1677:         """C8 FIA V1 TND for prefill states (PrefillNoCache uses float KV directly,
1678:         PrefillCacheHit gathers + dequants paged INT8 KV).
1679:         """
1680:         key, value, block_size, block_table, actual_seq_lengths_kv = self._get_fia_params(key, value, attn_metadata)
1681: 
1682:         actual_seq_qlen = attn_metadata.actual_seq_lengths_q
1683:         num_tokens = int(actual_seq_qlen[-1])  # type: ignore[index]
1684:         query = query[:num_tokens]
1685: 
1686:         if (
1687:             attn_metadata.attn_state == AscendAttentionState.PrefillNoCache
1688:             and self.attn_type != AttentionType.ENCODER_DECODER
1689:         ):
1690:             key = key[:num_tokens]
1691:             value = value[:num_tokens]
1692: 
1693:         if key.dtype == torch.int8:
1694:             if block_table is not None:
1695:                 seq_lens = (
1696:                     actual_seq_lengths_kv if isinstance(actual_seq_lengths_kv, list) else actual_seq_lengths_kv.tolist()
1697:                 )
1698:                 key, value = self._dequant_paged_kv_to_dense(key, value, block_table, seq_lens, query.dtype, layer)
1699:                 block_table = None
1700:                 # block_table is None after dequant; FIA ignores block_size.
1701:                 # Use cache block_size for consistency rather than a magic number.
1702:                 block_size = self.key_cache.shape[1]  # type: ignore[attr-defined]
1703:                 actual_seq_lengths_kv = torch.tensor(seq_lens, dtype=torch.int32).cumsum(dim=0)
1704:             else:
1705:                 key = (key.to(query.dtype) - layer._c8_k_offset) * layer._c8_k_scale
1706:                 value = (value.to(query.dtype) - layer._c8_v_offset) * layer._c8_v_scale
1707: 
1708:         attn_output, _ = torch_npu.npu_fused_infer_attention_score(
1709:             query=query,
1710:             key=key,
1711:             value=value,
1712:             atten_mask=attn_metadata.attn_mask,
1713:             block_table=block_table,
1714:             input_layout="TND",
1715:             block_size=block_size,
1716:             actual_seq_lengths=actual_seq_qlen,
1717:             actual_seq_lengths_kv=actual_seq_lengths_kv,
1718:             num_key_value_heads=self.num_kv_heads,
1719:             num_heads=self.num_heads,
1720:             scale=self.scale,
1721:             sparse_mode=3,
1722:         )
1723:         attn_output = attn_output.view(num_tokens, self.num_heads, self.head_size)
1724:         output[:num_tokens] = attn_output
1725:         return output

982:     def _get_fia_params(self, key: torch.Tensor, value: torch.Tensor, attn_metadata: AscendMetadata, kv_cache=None):
983:         # PrefillNoCache doesn't need key_cache, but other modes do
984:         # Only initialize/require cache for modes that actually use it
985:         if attn_metadata.attn_state != AscendAttentionState.PrefillNoCache:
986:             # Initialize cache from kv_cache if not already set (for DecodeOnly mode)
987:             if self.key_cache is None and kv_cache is not None:
988:                 if (
989:                     isinstance(kv_cache, torch.Tensor)
990:                     and kv_cache.dim() > 0
991:                     and kv_cache.shape[0] == 2
992:                     or isinstance(kv_cache, (list, tuple))
993:                     and len(kv_cache) >= 2
994:                 ):
995:                     self.key_cache, self.value_cache = kv_cache[0], kv_cache[1]
996: 
997:             if self.key_cache is None:

999:                     f"key_cache is None in _get_fia_params for mode {attn_metadata.attn_state}. kv_cache={kv_cache}"
1000:                 )
1001: 
1002:         if attn_metadata.attn_state == AscendAttentionState.PrefillNoCache:
1003:             block_size = 128
1004:             block_table = None
1005:             actual_seq_lengths_kv = attn_metadata.actual_seq_lengths_q
1006:             if self.attn_type == AttentionType.ENCODER_DECODER:
1007:                 actual_seq_lengths_kv = torch.cumsum(attn_metadata.seq_lens, dim=0).tolist()
1008:         elif attn_metadata.attn_state == AscendAttentionState.PrefillCacheHit:

1029:         # chunked prefill.

1031:             num_block, block_size, _, _ = self.key_cache.shape  # type: ignore

1033:                 num_block, block_size, -1
1034:             )
1035:             value = self.value_cache.view(  # type: ignore
1036:                 num_block, block_size, -1
1037:             )
1038:             block_table = attn_metadata.block_tables

1040:         return key, value, block_size, block_table, actual_seq_lengths_kv
1041: 
1042:     def forward_fused_infer_attention(
1043:         self,
1044:         query: torch.Tensor,
1045:         key: torch.Tensor,
1046:         value: torch.Tensor,
1047:         attn_metadata: AscendMetadata,
1048:         output: torch.Tensor,
1049:         kv_cache=None,
1050:     ):
1051:         # we inherit ForwardContext in model runner v2, when enable model
1052:         # runner v2, there is not capturing attribute in forward_context,
1053:         # just use getattr to avoid attribute error.
1054:         if _EXTRA_CTX.capturing:
1055:             if self.sinks is not None:
1056:                 attn_output, num_tokens = self.full_graph_fia_v2(query, key, value, attn_metadata, output)
1057:                 output[:num_tokens] = attn_output[:num_tokens]
1058:                 return output
1059:             else:
1060:                 attn_output, num_tokens = self.full_graph_fia(query, key, value, attn_metadata, output)
1061:                 output[:num_tokens] = attn_output[:num_tokens]
1062:                 return output
1063:         passed_key = key
1064:         key, value, block_size, block_table, actual_seq_lengths_kv = self._get_fia_params(
1065:             key, value, attn_metadata, kv_cache
1066:         )
1067:         if self.enable_hamming_sparse and attn_metadata.attn_state != AscendAttentionState.DecodeOnly:
1068:             reshape_and_cache_kvcomp(attn_metadata.kvcomp_metadata, self.layerIndex, passed_key)
1069:         elif self.enable_hamming_sparse:
1070:             block_table, actual_seq_lengths_kv = get_kvcomp_decode_params(
1071:                 self.layerIndex, attn_metadata.kvcomp_metadata, query, passed_key, block_table, actual_seq_lengths_kv
1072:             )
1073:         num_tokens = attn_metadata.actual_seq_lengths_q[-1]
1074:         query = query[:num_tokens]
1075:         if (
1076:             attn_metadata.attn_state == AscendAttentionState.PrefillNoCache
1077:             and self.attn_type != AttentionType.ENCODER_DECODER
1078:         ):
1079:             key = key[:num_tokens]
1080:             value = value[:num_tokens]
1081:         # Get workspace from cache or calculate it if not present.
1082:         if self.sinks is not None:
1083:             actual_seq_qlen = attn_metadata.actual_seq_lengths_q
1084:             if attn_metadata.attn_state == AscendAttentionState.DecodeOnly:
1085:                 actual_seq_qlen = torch.tensor([1] * len(attn_metadata.seq_lens_list), dtype=torch.int32).cumsum(dim=0)
1086:             if self.sliding_window is not None:
1087:                 sparse_mode = 4
1088:             else:
1089:                 sparse_mode = 3
1090:             attn_output, _ = torch_npu.npu_fused_infer_attention_score_v2(
1091:                 query,
1092:                 key,
1093:                 value,
1094:                 num_query_heads=self.num_heads,
1095:                 num_key_value_heads=self.num_kv_heads,
1096:                 input_layout="TND",
1097:                 pre_tokens=self.sliding_window if self.sliding_window is not None else SWA_INT_MAX,
1098:                 next_tokens=0,
1099:                 atten_mask=attn_metadata.attn_mask,
1100:                 sparse_mode=sparse_mode,
1101:                 softmax_scale=self.scale,
1102:                 block_table=block_table,
1103:                 block_size=block_size,
1104:                 actual_seq_qlen=actual_seq_qlen,
1105:                 actual_seq_kvlen=actual_seq_lengths_kv,
1106:                 learnable_sink=self.sinks,
1107:             )
1108:         else:
1109:             if not attn_metadata.causal:
1110:                 attn_output, _ = torch_npu.npu_fused_infer_attention_score(
1111:                     query=query,
1112:                     key=key,

1255:     def forward_impl(

1276:     def forward(
1277:         self,
1278:         layer: AttentionLayer,
1279:         query: torch.Tensor,
1280:         key: torch.Tensor,
1281:         value: torch.Tensor,

1290:             query: shape = [num_tokens, num_heads, head_size]

1292:             value: shape = [num_tokens, num_kv_heads, head_size]

1298:         """
